moveAnnotations.annotation: We start with a quiet flank move that defines the Shy Attack—an unusual but solid opening where we avoid central confrontation early on. The a3 pawn prepares to support a potential b4 advance later and gives our king's rook flexibility on the a-file, though our real plan unfolds slowly on the kingside with g3 and a fianchetto setup.

Black immediately claims space in the center, which is natural and strong. By playing e5, your opponent controls d4 and f4, preventing you from easily establishing a pawn there and forcing you to decide whether to challenge this central presence or work around it.

Now we commit to our fianchetto plan on the kingside, preparing to develop the bishop to g2 where it will long-diagonal pressure toward d5 and exert influence over the center from afar. This move is characteristically "shy"—we're building a solid, flexible position rather than fighting directly for central squares.

Black advances the d-pawn to support the e5 center and gains even more space, challenging your patience. Your opponent is building a strong pawn chain and preventing you from easily playing e4 yourself, so you must now decide whether to accept a cramped position or find ways to create counterplay.

We complete our fianchetto setup, placing the bishop on its ideal long diagonal where it watches d5, c4, and potentially all the way to a8. The bishop on g2 becomes one of the most important pieces in our position, providing long-range influence and preventing Black from feeling too comfortable with their central space advantage.

Black develops naturally, bringing the knight toward the center and preparing to support the kingside or reroute to active squares. This is solid development that adds pressure to your center, but you should remember that Nf6 also slightly weakens the e-file and kingside, which could become relevant later.

We solidify the center and prepare to support e3, creating a stable pawn structure that prevents Black from breaking through with tactical shots. The d3 pawn also opens the c1-h6 diagonal for our light-squared bishop (eventually) and supports our kingside expansion plans—this slow, methodical approach is the essence of the Shy Attack.

Black develops the queenside knight, which controls d4 and e4, making it harder for you to expand there. This is typical developing play, though now Black's pieces are becoming active and you need to catch up with your own development while maintaining your patient, solid structure.

We develop toward the center while keeping our options open—from d2, the knight can jump to f3 (supporting the kingside), e4 (challenging Black's center), or c4 (attacking the center and queenside). This is more flexible than Nf3 would be right now, and Nd2 also prepares to support a potential e4 break later.

Black places the bishop actively on d6, eyeing your kingside and preparing to support a potential kingside attack or simply maintaining flexibility. This move suggests your opponent may be planning h6, Be6, or f5 to crack open your kingside, so you should begin thinking about defensive prophylaxis soon.

We shore up the center and prepare f4 to challenge Black's strong e5 pawn while maintaining our fianchettoed bishop's long-diagonal control. The e3 pawn also creates a flexible structure where we can support d4 advances if needed, and it keeps our kingside solid against any sudden Black attacks.

Black castles kingside to safety, connecting the rooks and tucking the king away—a logical choice that completes their development on the kingside. However, castling also commits Black's king position, which means your slow kingside preparations (like h3 next) gain real point if you can later play f4 or g4 with attacking intentions.

We prevent Black from playing Ng4 and initiating kingside tricks while quietly preparing a potential g4 advance to expand on the kingside. This prophylactic move is quintessentially "Shy Attack"—we're not attacking yet, but we're denying Black counterplay and keeping our kingside secure while building slow pressure.
